title: Anesthesia for Immune Mediated and Infectious Diseases MCQ – Chapter 25
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: What is the underlying pathology of rheumatoid arthritis?
questions.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis is a disease characterized by immune-mediated synovitis.

questions.question: Which associated comorbidities should be ruled out in rheumatoid arthritis patients preoperatively?

options.B: Cardiac valvular lesions, pericarditis, and pulmonary fibrosis

questions.explanation: Associated comorbidities like cardiac valvular lesions, pericarditis, and pulmonary fibrosis should be ruled out.

questions.question: What is the most important concern in anesthetic management of rheumatoid arthritis?
questions.explanation: As difficult airway is the most important concern, airway evaluation should be done very meticulously.

questions.question: Which side effects should be considered preoperatively in rheumatoid arthritis patients?

options.B: Side effects of DMARDs, NSAIDs, and corticosteroids

questions.explanation: Side effects of disease modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs), NSAIDs and corticosteroids should be taken into consideration.

questions.question: What is the preferred choice of anesthesia in rheumatoid arthritis patients?
questions.explanation: Regional anesthesia is the choice. Lumbar spine is usually not involved in rheumatoid arthritis therefore spinal/epidural can be given easily.

questions.question: Why is the lumbar spine usually suitable for spinal/epidural in rheumatoid arthritis?

options.B: Lumbar spine is usually not involved in rheumatoid arthritis

questions.explanation: Lumbar spine is usually not involved in rheumatoid arthritis therefore spinal/epidural can be given easily.

questions.question: Why is general anesthesia difficult and risky in rheumatoid arthritis patients?
questions.explanation: General anesthesia is difficult and risky due to difficult intubation caused by atlantoaxial instability, stiffness of cervical spine, and temporomandibular and cricoarytenoid arthritis.

questions.question: What can happen if the neck is flexed in patients with atlantoaxial subluxation?

options.B: Odontoid process can compress spinal cord leading to quadriplegia

questions.explanation: Flexion of neck can cause odontoid process to compress spinal cord leading to quadriplegia.

questions.question: How should intubation be performed in rheumatoid arthritis patients with atlantoaxial subluxation?

options.A: Rapid sequence intubation with neck flexion
options.B: Awake intubation under topical anesthesia, or under GA with neck in extension

questions.explanation: Intubation is best performed under topical anesthesia with awake patient. If under GA, intubation should be done with neck in extension.

questions.question: What are the three causes of difficult intubation in rheumatoid arthritis?

options.B: Atlantoaxial instability, stiffness of cervical spine, temporomandibular and cricoarytenoid arthritis
options.C: Tracheal stenosis, vocal cord paralysis, subglottic edema

questions.explanation: Difficult intubation is due to atlantoaxial instability, stiffness of cervical spine, and temporomandibular and cricoarytenoid arthritis.

questions.question: Why may arterial cannulation be difficult in rheumatoid arthritis patients?
questions.explanation: Arterial cannulation may be difficult due to calcified radial arteries.

questions.question: Why may central venous cannulation be difficult in rheumatoid arthritis?

options.C: Due to superior vena cava obstruction

questions.explanation: Central venous cannulation may be difficult due to cervical spine fusion.

questions.question: Why can ankylosing spondylitis be the most difficult case from anesthesia point of view?

options.B: Fusion of lumbar spine makes CNB impossible and fusion of cervical spine makes intubation impossible

questions.explanation: Fusion of lumbar spine may make central neuraxial blocks impossible and fusion of cervical spine may make intubation impossible.

questions.question: How should intubation be performed in ankylosing spondylitis patients with cervical spine fusion?
questions.explanation: Intubation in such patients should be performed in awake state under fiberoptic bronchoscopy.

questions.question: Which cardiac conditions are common in ankylosing spondylitis?

options.B: Aortic regurgitation and bundle branch block

questions.explanation: Aortic regurgitation and bundle branch block are common in ankylosing spondylitis.

questions.question: What determines the perioperative morbidity in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)?
questions.explanation: The involvement of major organ functions determines the perioperative morbidity in SLE.

questions.question: What laryngeal involvement in SLE can make intubation difficult?

options.B: Cricoarytenoid arthritis and laryngeal nerve palsy

questions.explanation: Laryngeal involvement including cricoarytenoid arthritis and laryngeal nerve palsy can make intubation difficult in SLE.

questions.question: Which organ involvement in scleroderma significantly affects perioperative morbidity?
questions.explanation: Multiorgan involvement, particularly pulmonary fibrosis and pulmonary hypertension can significantly affect perioperative morbidity.

questions.question: Why can intubation be difficult in scleroderma?
questions.explanation: Small oral aperture can make intubation difficult in scleroderma.

questions.question: Why can IV cannulation be difficult in scleroderma?
questions.explanation: Dermal thickening can make intravenous cannulation difficult in scleroderma.

questions.question: How are HIV positive patients classified according to ASA grading?
questions.explanation: Due to involvement of multiple organs all HIV positive patients are categorized as ASA grade II.

questions.question: How are patients with AIDS classified according to ASA grading?
questions.explanation: Patients with AIDS are categorized as ASA III/IV.

questions.question: Which side effects of HAART should be ruled out preoperatively?

options.B: Diabetes, coronary and cerebrovascular disease, deranged liver function

questions.explanation: Side effects of HAART including diabetes, coronary and cerebrovascular disease, deranged liver function must be ruled out preoperatively.

questions.question: Patients who started HAART within how many months of surgery suffer more from perioperative complications?
questions.explanation: Patients who have started HAART within 6 months of surgery suffer more from perioperative complications.

questions.question: Do CD4 count and viral load increase perioperative anesthetic complications?

options.B: Yes, but only in emergency surgery
options.C: No, they affect overall prognosis but not perioperative anesthetic complications

questions.explanation: CD4 count and viral load do affect the overall prognosis after surgery but have not been found to increase perioperative anesthetic complications.

questions.question: Can regional anesthesia be given to HIV/AIDS patients?

options.B: Yes, safely if there are no associated neuropathies

questions.explanation: Regional anesthesia can be given safely if there are no associated neuropathies.
questions.topic: HIV and AIDS – Choice of Anesthesia

questions.question: What can make intubation difficult in HIV/AIDS patients?
questions.explanation: Fat redistribution around the neck can make intubation difficult in HIV/AIDS patients.

questions.question: What is vital during any procedure in HIV/AIDS patients?

options.B: Maintaining universal precautions and high degree of asepsis

questions.explanation: Maintaining universal precautions and high degree of asepsis during any procedure is vital.

questions.question: In rheumatoid arthritis, intubation should be performed with the neck in which position?
questions.explanation: Due to atlantoaxial instability, intubation in RA patients should be performed with neck in extension.

questions.question: Which joint involvement in rheumatoid arthritis contributes to difficult intubation besides atlantoaxial and cervical spine?
questions.explanation: Temporomandibular and cricoarytenoid arthritis contribute to difficult intubation in rheumatoid arthritis.

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about ankylosing spondylitis and anesthesia?

options.B: General anesthesia with direct laryngoscopy is preferred
options.C: It may turn out to be the most difficult case from anesthesia point of view

questions.explanation: From anesthesia point of view ankylosing spondylitis may turn out to be the most difficult case at times.

questions.question: In ankylosing spondylitis, fusion of which spine makes central neuraxial blocks impossible?
questions.explanation: Fusion of lumbar spine may make central neuraxial blocks impossible.

questions.question: In ankylosing spondylitis, fusion of which spine makes intubation impossible?
questions.explanation: Fusion of cervical spine may make intubation impossible in ankylosing spondylitis.

questions.question: What type of valvular disease is commonly associated with ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation: Aortic regurgitation is common in ankylosing spondylitis.

questions.question: What conduction abnormality is common in ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation: Bundle branch block is common in ankylosing spondylitis.

questions.question: Which condition is characterized by immune-mediated synovitis?
questions.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis is a disease characterized by immune-mediated synovitis.

questions.question: In which condition can small oral aperture cause difficult intubation?
questions.explanation: In scleroderma, small oral aperture can make intubation difficult.

questions.question: Which condition can cause cricoarytenoid arthritis leading to difficult intubation — BOTH rheumatoid arthritis AND SLE?

options.A: True – both RA and SLE can cause cricoarytenoid arthritis

questions.explanation: Both rheumatoid arthritis (temporomandibular and cricoarytenoid arthritis) and SLE (laryngeal involvement including cricoarytenoid arthritis) can cause difficult intubation.

questions.question: Which is the best method of intubation in a patient with atlantoaxial subluxation under general anesthesia?
questions.explanation: If intubation has to be done under general anesthesia, it should be done with neck in extension to avoid odontoid compression of spinal cord.

questions.question: Patients with atlantoaxial subluxation cannot tolerate which neck movement?
questions.explanation: Patients of atlantoaxial subluxation cannot tolerate flexion as it can cause odontoid process to compress spinal cord.

questions.question: What is HAART in the context of HIV management?
questions.explanation: HAART stands for Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy used in HIV management.

questions.question: Which pulmonary conditions in scleroderma affect perioperative outcome?
questions.explanation: Pulmonary fibrosis and pulmonary hypertension in scleroderma significantly affect perioperative morbidity.

questions.question: What is the awake intubation technique of choice for ankylosing spondylitis with cervical spine fusion?
questions.explanation: Intubation should be performed in awake state under fiberoptic bronchoscopy in ankylosing spondylitis patients.

questions.question: Why are HIV positive patients classified as at least ASA II?

options.A: Due to high risk of bleeding
options.B: Due to involvement of multiple organs

questions.explanation: Due to involvement of multiple organs all HIV positive patients are categorized as ASA grade II.

questions.question: Which condition among the immune mediated diseases can cause laryngeal nerve palsy?
questions.explanation: In SLE, laryngeal involvement including laryngeal nerve palsy can make intubation difficult.

questions.question: What is the cause of fat redistribution around the neck in HIV/AIDS patients?
questions.explanation: Fat redistribution (lipodystrophy) is a known side effect of HAART which can cause fat accumulation around the neck making intubation difficult.

questions.question: Which immune mediated disease most commonly involves the cervical spine causing intubation difficulty?
questions.explanation: Both RA (atlantoaxial instability, cervical spine stiffness) and ankylosing spondylitis (cervical spine fusion) commonly involve the cervical spine causing difficult intubation.

questions.question: In which condition should neuropathies be ruled out before giving regional anesthesia?
questions.explanation: In HIV/AIDS patients, regional anesthesia can be given safely if there are no associated neuropathies.
questions.topic: HIV and AIDS – Choice of Anesthesia

questions.question: Pericarditis is an associated comorbidity of which immune mediated disease?
questions.explanation: Associated comorbidities like cardiac valvular lesions, pericarditis, and pulmonary fibrosis should be ruled out in rheumatoid arthritis.

questions.question: Pulmonary fibrosis is a comorbidity associated with which TWO immune mediated diseases from this chapter?
questions.explanation: Pulmonary fibrosis is mentioned as a comorbidity of both rheumatoid arthritis (preoperative evaluation) and scleroderma (multiorgan involvement).
